label: ai
topic: Men who confide their trauma to you — loneliness and male vulnerability
text:
I read recently that fifteen percent of American men have no close friends. Zero. Nobody to call when the basement floods or the marriage suffocates. It is a sobering statistical fact. It is a personal fact that all fifteen percent of them seem to live in my neighborhood, and they all want to talk to me while holding power tools.

There is something about my face that invites a terrifying level of masculine unburdening. I am a human diary with a padlock they know how to pick. It usually starts with an object. A lawnmower, a wrench, a bag of charcoal.

Take my neighbor, Ed. Last July, I walked to the fence to borrow his hedge clippers. Ed is sixty-two, owns three aggressively polished sedans, and wears New Balance sneakers with an utter lack of irony. He handed me the clippers. The motor was still vibrating.

"You gotta oil the blades," he said, staring at my shoulder. "Keep 'em sharp."

"Will do, Ed. Thanks."

"My wife had a miscarriage in 1998," he said. Just like that. The clippers hummed in my hands. The smell of severed pine needles filled the humid air. He didn't look at my eyes, only at the rusted chain-link between us. "It was a boy. We had the room painted blue. Sometimes I just sit in the garage and think about how he'd be out of college by now."

I stood there, holding a lethal gardening tool, entirely unequipped for the sudden, gaping wound he had presented to me over the property line. "I'm so sorry, Ed," I murmured. He nodded, wiped a smudge of grease from his chin, and walked back to his driveway without another word.

Men are taught to build walls, thick and windowless. But water always finds a crack. When they finally locate someone who will not mock them, they don't just open a door; they detonate the entire foundation.

At a Super Bowl party three years ago, a casual friend named Mark cornered me by the kitchen island. He was holding a plastic cup of stale IPA and wearing a jersey. He asked me to pass the onion dip. Then he asked if I ever drove over the interstate bridge and had the overwhelming, magnetic urge to jerk the steering wheel to the right and plunge into the river. "Just to see what it feels like to stop," he whispered, dipping a ruffled potato chip. I spent the next two hours missing the game, sitting with him on the back porch, watching him cry into the sleeves of his oversized jersey. Statistics say men die by suicide at four times the rate of women. Mark was a walking decimal point, begging to be rounded up to whole.

Even my father, a man whose emotional vocabulary consists entirely of nodding at the Weather Channel, succumbed. We were changing a flat tire on my Honda. He was kneeling on the asphalt, struggling with the lug nut. The tire iron slipped, scraping his knuckles. He didn't curse. He just stared at the blood welling on his skin.

"Your Uncle David bled out, you know," he said softly. His brother had drank himself to death a decade prior. Liver failure. "I should have called him more. I didn't know what to say. So I said nothing."

He tightened the nut with a violent, wrenching twist. I reached down and put my hand on his shoulder. He didn't brush it off. We stayed like that on the side of the road, the cars rushing past us, two men tethered by a touch, terrified of the silence, desperate for the gravity of another human hand.
